title: CVA6 Sv39 virtual-address check off-by-one
tagline: An off-by-one in a production RISC-V virtual-memory check.
hook_30s: This is the RISC-V Sv39 spec: virtual-address bits 63 down to 38 must all equal bit 38. This is CVA6's code: it checks bits 63 down to 39. That single off-by-one shipped in a virtual-memory protection check in a production RISC-V core. ChipGPT finds it from the spec in 14 seconds.
category: 25 — spec-to-RTL drift (the #1 root cause of respins)

proof.note: Combinational spec-drift — proven exhaustively by formal (SymbiYosys counterexample) and reproduced by a directed test.

title: Hack@DAC 2021: AES CTR counter never increments
tagline: One missing +1 turns AES into a fixed keystream.
hook_30s: AES in counter mode requires a counter that increments every block. If it doesn't, your cipher becomes a one-time-pad with a fixed keystream — a known-plaintext attack recovers your secrets. This bug was inserted in a Hack@DAC SoC. ChipGPT finds it in 8 seconds.

proof.note: Open-source formal (SymbiYosys) proves it via the combinational next-state form — ctr_d must differ from ctr_q under the increment condition — and a 2-block directed test reproduces it. The textbook temporal $past form would need commercial SVA.

title: OpenTitan lc_ctrl -> KMAC CDC missing synchronizer
tagline: A clock-domain crossing your simulator can't see.
hook_30s: This bug shipped through RTL simulation and was found in gate-level simulation — hundreds of regression hours wasted. It's a clock-domain crossing inside OpenTitan's life-cycle controller, on the path to the cryptographic accelerator. ChipGPT finds it in 23 seconds, before GLS.

proof.note: Invisible to RTL simulation. Found by clock-domain (structural) analysis — not simulation, not functional formal.
